data.title: First industrial realty trust outlines 2025 FFO guidance amid leasing progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter Baccile highlighted strong leasing achievements, including a 95.3% in-service occupancy rate and a 30% overall cash rental rate increase for new and renewal leases, which rises to 36% when excluding a Central PA fixed-rate renewal. He noted that leasing progress on 2025 rollovers reached 73% by square footage.
  • The company announced plans to break ground on a 176,000 square-foot development in Northwest Dallas and a 226,000 square-foot project in Philadelphia during Q2 2025. Both projects target underserved market segments and are expected to deliver cash yields of approximately 8%.
  • On the capital front, the company renewed a $200 million term loan and expanded its revolving credit facility by $100 million, extending maturities into 2030.
  • CFO Scott Musil reported NAREIT FFO of $0.68 per diluted share for Q1 2025, up from $0.60 in Q1 2024. He stated that cash same-store NOI growth was 10.1% for the quarter, supported by rental rate increases and higher average occupancy.

Outlook

  • Guidance for full-year 2025 NAREIT FFO remains unchanged at $2.87 to $2.97 per share. Key assumptions include average in-service occupancy of 95% to 96% and cash same-store NOI growth of 6% to 7%.
  • Management expects in-service occupancy to bottom in Q2 2025 before improving by year-end, supported by anticipated development leasing in the fourth quarter.
  • The updated guidance also incorporates costs related to completed and under-construction developments, as well as new starts in Q2 2025.

Financial Results

  • Same-store NOI growth excluding termination fees reached 10.1% in Q1 2025.
  • Leasing activity included 1.3 million square feet of leases commencing during the quarter, comprising 400,000 square feet of new leases and 800,000 square feet of renewals.
  • The company extended debt maturities, with its next significant maturity now scheduled for 2027.

Q&A

  • Ki Bin Kim, Truist, asked about risks related to tariffs and tenant exposure to Chinese 3PLs. CIO Jojo Yap noted that such tenants occupy only 450,000 square feet, representing minimal exposure.
  • Craig Mailman, Citi, inquired about development leasing assumptions. CFO Scott Musil clarified that 1.5 million square feet of development leasing is assumed for Q4 2025, with a potential $0.02 per share impact if not achieved.
  • Vikram Malhotra, Morgan Stanley, questioned the pace of leasing decision-making amid tariff uncertainties. CEO Peter Baccile indicated that while some conversations have paused, others remain active, particularly in regions unaffected by tariffs.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the impact of tariffs and leasing delays, with questions focusing on tenant demand, development leasing, and guidance assumptions. The tone was cautious yet inquisitive.
  • Management maintained a confident outlook, emphasizing the company’s ability to adapt to market conditions and execute its strategic objectives. CEO Baccile expressed optimism about leasing momentum, despite acknowledging potential headwinds.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained consistent, focusing on opportunities for long-term growth amidst near-term uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In-service occupancy declined slightly to 95.3% from 96.2% at year-end, reflecting typical seasonal patterns and ongoing development activity.
  • Leasing progress on 2025 rollovers improved from 59% to 73% by square footage.
  • Guidance for 2025 NAREIT FFO and cash same-store NOI growth remained unchanged, with no material changes to key assumptions.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general market conditions to specific risks related to tariffs and tenant decision-making timelines.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted geopolitical uncertainties related to tariffs as a potential risk, which could impact tenant demand and leasing decisions.
  • Analysts raised concerns about leasing velocity and the timing of development lease-ups, particularly in the context of macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Mitigation strategies include targeting underserved market segments and maintaining a diversified tenant base.

Final Takeaway

First Industrial Realty Trust began 2025 with solid leasing progress and strategic development initiatives targeting high-demand markets. While geopolitical uncertainties and leasing decision delays introduce risks, the company’s strong portfolio performance, robust financial position, and cautious guidance provide a stable outlook for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sweetgreen COO departs
data.description:
• Sweetgreen (NYSE:SG) said on Thursday that Rossann Williams, the company’s Chief Operating Officer, would leave her employment with the company, effective immediately.
  • Williams transitioned into a consulting role with the company as of that date, and will continue to advise the company through June 1, 2025.

data.title: Key takeaways from Netflix's first-quarter earnings, as stock gains
data.description:
Netflix stock (NASDAQ:NFLX) ticked 4% higher after hours following the release of its first-quarter earnings -- where the company beat expectations on top and bottom lines thanks to positive results both in subscriptions and advertising.

User-related financials

Revenue of $10.54B topped analyst consensus for $10.5B, and diluted earnings per share of $6.61 easily topped views for $5.68.

Though the company no longer reports quarterly subscriber additions, it noted that revenue (up 13% year-over-year) and operating income (up 27%, to $3.35B) both outpaced company guidance thanks to slightly higher subscription and advertising revenues, as well as some expense timing.

Operating margin also rose, to 31.7% from a year-ago 28.1% (and last quarter's 22.2%), and the company forecast another gain in the current quarter.

Monetization

Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) noted that recent large-market pricing adjustments (including the United States, UK and Argentina) have performed "in line with our expectations," and adds that today it's adjusting prices in France.

On April 1, the company rolled out its in-house first-party ad-tech platform, the Netflix Ads Suite, in the United States -- and it will bring it to other markets in the coming months.

"In Q1, we launched programmatic in EMEA, and now offer programmatic capability in UCAN, EMEA and LATAM, with a full APAC launch coming in Q2," the company said.

Cash flow

Free cash flow rose to $2.66B from a year-ago total of $2.14B, and from last quarter's $1.38B. Net cash from operations rose to $2.79B from a year-ago $2.21B and last quarter's $1.54B.

The company's still forecasting full-year free cash flow of about $8B, and noted during Q1 it paid down $800M in senior notes thanks to a 2024 refinancing, and bought back 3.7M shares for $3.5B (leaving $13.6B still authorized for repurchase).

"Our capital allocation approach is unchanged - we prioritize profitable growth by reinvesting in our business, maintaining ample liquidity, and returning excess cash (beyond several billion dollars of minimum cash and any used for selective M&A) to shareholders through share repurchases," the company noted.

Guidance

Netflix (NFLX) had previously said that management would no longer report quarterly subscriber additions or average revenue per subscriber. 

But it is forecasting second-quarter revenue growth of 15%, which would equate to $11.04B vs. expectations for $10.9B. That guidance comes "as we see the full quarter benefit fromrecent price changes and continued growth in membership and advertising revenue. We projectoperating margin of 33%, a ~6 percentage point year-over-year improvement."

And it reiterated full-year revenue expectations for $43.5B-$44.5B, vs. consensus for $44.29B, as well as full-year operating margin of 29%.

The company's usual Q&A interview with executives is set to begin at 4:45 p.m. ET.
